worth_it_explanation: Structure decisions on LinkedIn set what the account optimizes toward and how clearly it can be read and budgeted, so a structure problem touches both performance and reporting. The check is quick to run inside Campaign Manager. The objective each campaign uses is visible in its settings, and the campaign-group layout is visible from the account's top level. Neither requires a rebuild to fix because an objective change is a new campaign and grouping is an organizational move, and both pay off as soon as they are in place.
intro: LinkedIn campaigns sit inside campaign groups under the ad account, and the objective each campaign uses sets what LinkedIn optimizes toward and which ad formats and bidding strategies are available. These checks confirm that each objective matches the funnel stage its campaign serves, and that campaigns are grouped rather than left flat at one level. Both shape how the account is optimized, budgeted, and read.

audit_checks.title: Campaign objectives are mismatched to their funnel stage
audit_checks.impact_rationale: The objective decides what LinkedIn optimizes delivery toward, so a campaign meant to drive leads that runs on a Brand Awareness objective optimizes for reach rather than form fills. Spend goes to the wrong outcome, and the campaign's results read against a goal that does not match what the campaign was built to do.

educate.base:
On LinkedIn, the campaign objective is the first setting you choose when you build a campaign, and it is the basic intent of the campaign. The objective decides what LinkedIn optimizes delivery toward, and it also filters which ad formats and bidding strategies the campaign can use. LinkedIn offers seven primary objectives, including Brand Awareness, Website Visits, Engagement, Video Views, Lead Generation, and Website Conversions, per LinkedIn's campaign objectives documentation.

A mismatch happens when the objective does not match the stage the campaign is meant to serve. A Brand Awareness objective optimizes toward reach and impressions, so a campaign built to capture leads that runs on Brand Awareness has LinkedIn delivering to the people most likely to see the ad rather than the people most likely to fill in a form. The ad spends against the wrong signal, and the reporting frames the results against a goal the campaign was not designed for.

The objective also gates the optimization options. Lead Generation enables lead-optimized goals such as maximizing leads, and Website Conversions enables conversion-optimized delivery that depends on a conversion being associated with the campaign. A campaign sitting on an upper-funnel objective never reaches those lower-funnel optimization paths, even when its real goal is a conversion.

You may want to confirm each campaign's objective against the stage it serves. A few questions help surface a mismatch:
  • Does an upper-funnel objective sit on a campaign whose real goal is a lead or a sale?
  • Does a conversion or lead objective lack the conversion or lead form it needs to optimize?
  • Do campaigns aimed at the same goal use different objectives without a reason?

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a considered or business-to-business store, the lower-funnel objective is usually Website Conversions, which optimizes toward a tracked purchase or signup. You may want to confirm that a store's bottom-funnel campaigns run on Website Conversions with a conversion associated, rather than on Website Visits, which optimizes only toward landing-page clicks.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: Lead capture is the dominant case on LinkedIn, and it has two matching objectives. Lead Generation uses an in-platform form, and Website Conversions sends users to a form on the site. You may want to confirm that a lead campaign uses one of these rather than an upper-funnel objective such as Engagement or Brand Awareness, which optimize toward interactions and reach instead of leads.

learn_more.source_title: Understanding campaign settings in Campaign Manager

investigate.intro: Confirm in Campaign Manager that each campaign's objective matches the funnel stage it serves and supports the optimization the goal needs.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: Open Campaign Manager and select the ad account, then open the campaigns view from the left navigation.
For each campaign, note its objective. The objective appears in the campaign list and in the campaign's settings when you open it.
Match each objective against the stage the campaign is meant to serve. An upper-funnel objective such as Brand Awareness on a campaign built to drive leads or sales is a mismatch to record.
For any Website Conversions or Lead Generation campaign, confirm a conversion or a lead form is associated because those objectives need one to optimize toward the goal.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign, the objective and the funnel stage the campaign is meant to serve. A campaign whose objective matches its stage, and which has the conversion or lead form its objective needs, passes. A campaign running an objective that does not match its stage, or a conversion or lead objective with nothing to optimize toward, is a fail recorded with the campaign name and the mismatch.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the objective and the intended funnel stage for each campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more campaigns on {{site_name}} run an objective that does not match the funnel stage the campaign serves. The objective decides what LinkedIn optimizes delivery toward, so a lower-funnel campaign that runs on an upper-funnel objective such as Brand Awareness has its spend optimized for reach instead of the lead or sale it was built to drive. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each campaign's objective against its goal and rebuilding the mismatched campaigns under the objective that fits the stage, then confirming the new campaign has the conversion or lead form its objective needs to optimize.

audit_checks.title: A flat account has no campaign-group segmentation
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When every campaign sits at one level with no grouping, budget and reporting have no structure to follow, so the account is harder to read and to steer. Spend cannot be set or shifted at a group level, and performance has to be pieced together campaign by campaign rather than rolled up by the way the account is actually managed.

educate.base:
On LinkedIn, campaign groups are the layer between the ad account and individual campaigns. A campaign group holds related campaigns so they can be managed together, with a shared schedule, a group-level budget, and status control that applies to every campaign in the group, per LinkedIn's campaign groups documentation.

A flat account is one where this layer is unused. Every campaign sits in a single default group, or in groups that carry no meaning, so the structure does not mirror how the account is run. The account still functions, but the grouping that would let you budget and read it by funnel stage, audience, or initiative is not there.

The cost shows up in two places. Budget control is weaker because a group budget and schedule can govern a set of related campaigns at once rather than each campaign on its own. Reporting is harder to read because results have to be assembled campaign by campaign instead of rolling up to a group that represents a stage, a region, or a line of business.

A few signs point to a flat structure:
  • Most or all campaigns sit in the default campaign group
  • Campaign groups exist but do not map to a funnel stage, an audience, or a budget line
  • Related campaigns that share a goal are spread across unrelated groups
You may want to confirm whether the grouping reflects how the account is managed and reported, rather than whether groups merely exist.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a considered or business-to-business store, grouping by funnel stage keeps prospecting and remarketing budgets separate, which matters when a store wants to protect bottom-funnel spend. You may want to confirm that a store's campaigns are grouped so the conversion-focused work can be budgeted and read apart from the awareness work.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For lead capture, grouping by audience segment or by funnel stage lets a single group budget govern a set of lead campaigns that share a goal. You may want to confirm that lead campaigns aimed at the same segment sit together rather than spread across unrelated groups, so the segment's spend and results can be managed as one.

investigate.intro: Confirm in Campaign Manager whether campaigns are grouped in a way that mirrors how the account is budgeted and reported.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: Open Campaign Manager and select the ad account, then open the campaign groups view from the left navigation.
Note how many campaign groups exist and how many campaigns sit in each. A single group holding every campaign, or a default group with most of the campaigns, points to a flat structure.
For each group, check whether its name and contents map to a funnel stage, an audience, or a budget line. Groups that exist but carry no organizing logic count as flat.
Note any group that sets a group budget or schedule because a group with shared budget control is a sign the layer is being used rather than left empty.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the number of campaign groups, how campaigns are distributed across them, and whether each group maps to a funnel stage, audience, or budget line. An account whose groups reflect how it is managed and reported passes. An account where most campaigns sit in one or a default group, or whose groups carry no organizing logic, is a fail recorded with the distribution observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the campaign groups and how campaigns are distributed across them to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s account has little or no campaign-group segmentation, with campaigns sitting at a single level rather than grouped by funnel stage, audience, or budget line. A flat structure makes the account harder to budget because a group budget and schedule cannot govern related campaigns at once, and harder to read because results have to be pieced together campaign by campaign. {{voice_recommend}} organizing campaigns into campaign groups that mirror how the account is managed and reported, so budget can be set at the group level and performance rolls up by the way the account actually runs.
